text: Hello, I'm sharing a horrible gallbladder on a BMI-Fifty-Eight who has tremendous medical problems and when they came in several months ago got a drain in the gallbladder. The drain actually needed to be replaced two separate times during the interval prior to cholecystectomy.
The surgeon begins by taking down omental adhesions from the liver edge, using ICG for guidance, though the gallbladder has not yet been identified.

The surgeon works on the medial aspect of the gallbladder, progressing towards the fundus on the lateral side. A small incision into the gallbladder reveals necrotic purulence and fibrinous material emanating from its lumen.

The surgeon continues to remove the omentum from the gallbladder, achieving full separation. The drain is removed to allow for proper retraction of the liver, with numerous adhesions above the liver being taken down to improve exposure.

The surgeon performs dissection around the gallbladder, opening the peritoneum on either side of the perceived cystic duct. The common duct is visible, but the cystic duct does not appear to be illuminated.

The surgeon attempts to focus on the cystic duct to achieve a normal critical view, noting that the cystic duct is not illuminated with ICG due to obstruction, which complicates the dissection process.

The surgeon encounters a small vessel while observing the common bile duct positioned down and away. The junction of the gallbladder and cystic duct becomes visible, and efforts are made to separate the gallbladder from the liver bed to achieve the critical view.

The surgeon identifies what is believed to be the cystic duct and creates a window behind it, revealing additional vessels in the area. Despite the lack of ICG lighting to confirm the duct and inability to achieve a critical view, the duct is not taken at this time.

The surgeon attempts to peel the gallbladder from the liver bed to achieve a critical view. Unable to identify the artery, the surgeon transects the gallbladder about a third of the way up, revealing that the bottom half lacks a lumen.

The surgeon identifies a solid structure that appears to be the artery, located higher up on the medial aspect. They attempt to tease it off the gallbladder wall and create a window behind it, employing a modified top-down approach to facilitate identification of the cystic duct.

The surgeon encounters crossing vessels superiorly while attempting to identify the cystic duct. After dividing the gallbladder above the suspected duct area, the surgeon compresses the structure, observing mucus and a small lumen, confirming it as the cystic duct, which is then clipped.
The surgeon proceeds to divide the identified artery and continues to detach the inflamed gallbladder from the liver bed.

The surgeon completes the dissection around the fibrotic capsule of the gallbladder, removing the last attachments. The surgical field is irrigated, and hemostasis is checked to ensure there is no bleeding.
A drain is visible in the surgical field. The common duct is identified, with the presumed cystic duct located beneath it. The surgical site appears intact, indicating no visible injury.
